title: Primary Keys: Every Row's ID
intro: Your team's app has a bug: when two users sign up with the same name, the app updates the wrong user's profile. Imagine a school with two students named 'John Smith' and no student IDs -- you'd never know which one you're talking about. The problem? The table is using the name column to identify users instead of a proper primary key.

teach.title: Primary Keys = Social Security Numbers
teach.body: Imagine a school with two students named 'John Smith.' If you just use names, you can't tell them apart. That's why schools assign student IDs -- unique numbers that identify each student. A **primary key** is the same idea: a unique identifier for each row in a table, so you can always refer to exactly one specific record.

teach.title: The License Plate
teach.body: Two people can own identical-looking red Toyota Camrys, but every car has a unique **license plate**. That plate never changes even if the car gets repainted or changes owners. Police, insurance companies, and parking garages all use the plate to identify exactly which car they mean. A primary key is your data's license plate -- a permanent, unique tag that removes all ambiguity.

teach.title: What Makes a Good Primary Key?
teach.body:
A primary key must be:

1. **Unique** -- no two rows can have the same value
2. **Not null** -- every row must have one
3. **Unchanging** -- it should never need to be updated

Most developers use an **auto-incrementing integer** (1, 2, 3, 4...) or a **UUID** (a random string like 'a1b2c3d4-e5f6-...'). Never use names, emails, or other real data as primary keys -- they can change or have duplicates.

teach.title: Creating a Table with a Primary Key
teach.annotation: SERIAL means the database auto-generates the next number. PRIMARY KEY means this column uniquely identifies each row. Two users named 'Alice' get different IDs.

teach.body: Primary keys are how your app knows which customer paid, which order shipped, and which subscription to renew. Without them, your vibecoded app would mix up users and payments -- and that is how you lose customers and money fast.

Every table needs a primary key -- a unique identifier for each row. Use auto-incrementing integers (SERIAL) or UUIDs. Never use real-world data like names or emails as primary keys because they can change or duplicate.

start.narrative: Users report a weird bug: when they update their profile, sometimes another user's profile changes instead. You look at the users table and discover it has no primary key -- the app identifies users by their name column. Two users are both named 'Alex Johnson.' When the app runs UPDATE users SET bio = 'new bio' WHERE name = 'Alex Johnson', what happens?

choices.text: Both Alex Johnsons get the same bio -- the WHERE clause matches two rows
Only the first Alex Johnson gets updated
The database throws an error because of the duplicate name

wrong-first.narrative: SQL has no concept of 'first' or 'second' row -- rows in a table have no inherent order. WHERE matches ALL rows that meet the condition. Both Alex Johnsons match, so both get updated.

choices.text: So both rows get modified because the WHERE matches both

wrong-error.narrative: The database won't error -- duplicate names are perfectly valid because the name column has no UNIQUE constraint. SQL will happily update every matching row, even if that's 100 rows. That's exactly the problem.

choices.text: So both Alex Johnsons get the same update

diagnose-fix.narrative: You agree the table needs a real primary key. Which column do you choose?

choices.text: Add a new id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY column -- auto-generated, unique, unchanging
Promote the email column to PRIMARY KEY -- emails are unique
Add a UNIQUE constraint to the name column and forbid duplicate names

alter-table.narrative: How do you add the new id column safely on a table that already has thousands of rows in production?

choices.text: ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY -- Postgres backfills sequential IDs for existing rows automatically
Drop the table, recreate it with id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, then re-insert all the data from a backup
Add the column nullable, run an UPDATE to assign random integers, then add PRIMARY KEY -- and hope nothing collides

update-callers.narrative: The id column exists. But the app code still runs UPDATE users SET bio = ? WHERE name = ?. The bug isn't fixed until the callers change too. What's your migration strategy?

choices.text: Update every WHERE-by-name caller to WHERE id = ?, deploy the app and the migration together, and add a code-review check that flags any new WHERE name = ?
Update only the profile-update endpoint (the one in the bug report) and leave the rest of the WHERE-by-name callers for later
Skip the code update -- just having the id column should be enough

verify.narrative: Migration deployed. Before you call the bug closed, how do you prove the fix actually works for the reported scenario?

choices.text: Reproduce the original bug in staging with two 'Alex Johnson' rows: one updates their bio, confirm only that row's bio changed, then add a regression test that asserts the same
Eyeball production: query both Alex Johnsons and check their bios look different now
Trust the migration and close the ticket -- the schema clearly has a primary key now

end-good.narrative: The staging repro shows exactly one Alex Johnson's bio changed -- the other is untouched. The regression test fails on the OLD code and passes on the new code, proving it would have caught the original bug. With the full caller migration plus the test, the bug CLASS (not just this bug) is gone.
end-good.feedback: You did the full job: diagnosed the root cause (no primary key), chose the right kind of key (auto-incrementing integer, not real-world data), migrated the schema safely, updated the callers, and verified with a regression test. Primary keys only protect you if the code actually uses them AND the test proves it.

end-mediocre.narrative: The two bios look different in production today, so you close the ticket. But you only updated the profile-update endpoint, and the email-verification flow still runs WHERE name = ? -- you just didn't trigger it during the eyeball check. The next time two users share a name and one verifies their email, the other's account gets verified instead.
end-mediocre.feedback: Eyeballing production verifies one moment in time, not a class of bug. A real repro in staging plus a regression test would have surfaced the other WHERE-by-name callers, because writing the test forces you to enumerate the scenarios. Manual verification only catches the bugs you remember to look for.

end-weak.narrative: The migration ran and the id column is populated, but no app code uses it and you ran no verification. Every UPDATE still runs WHERE name = ?, both Alex Johnsons still get the same bio update, and the original bug ships unchanged. You added a column and called it a fix.
end-weak.feedback: A primary key in the schema does nothing if the app keeps identifying rows by non-unique data, and skipping verification means you don't even know that. The schema, the queries, and the test are one system -- changing only one part is the same as changing nothing for the user-visible bug.
